title: IELTS Writing Task 2: The Complete Crash Course
subtitle: Master the essential strategies to achieve your target band score

lessons.title: Why Task 2 Matters
lessons.liz_intro: Task 2 is two-thirds of your writing score and where most candidates lose marks. The good news: it doesn't need to be intimidating. Let's start with what you'll master today.
lessons.liz_outro: Now we'll tackle the three problems that wreck most Task 2 essays — and the solutions that fix them.

slides.title: IELTS Writing Task 2: The Complete Crash Course
slides.subtitle: Master the essential strategies to achieve your target band score with confidence and clarity.

blocks.text: **Quick orientation:** IELTS Writing has two tasks. **Task 1 (20 minutes)** is a short data-summary report — chart, graph, map, or process diagram. **Task 2 (40 minutes)** is the argumentative essay. This entire guide focuses on Task 2, because Task 2 is worth **two-thirds of your Writing score** and is where most candidates lose the most marks.
Writing Task 2 represents **two-thirds** of your overall writing score, making it the most significant component of the writing test.
It's also the section that generates the most anxiety amongst test-takers and where candidates frequently struggle to achieve their required scores.

**The good news?** Task 2 doesn't need to be intimidating. There are fundamental principles that are quite straightforward to grasp. The challenge lies in practical application — which is why we'll demonstrate exactly what to do and how to verify you're doing it correctly.

cards.title: 01. Question Analysis
cards.body: Learn to identify question types and understand precisely what's being asked of you.

cards.title: 02. Essay Structure
cards.body: Master a simple, reliable framework that works for every question type.

cards.title: 03. Paragraph Development
cards.body: Discover the Birthday Cake Strategy for creating fully developed, high-scoring paragraphs.

cards.title: 04. Complete Framework
cards.body: Apply the 9-step system that consistently delivers Band 7, 8, and 9 results.

slides.title: Minimum 250 words. No exceptions.
slides.body: An essay under 250 words is automatically penalised under **Task Response** — it doesn't matter how brilliant the argument is. Aim for **270–310 words**: enough to give yourself a 20–60 word safety buffer, not so long that you sacrifice precision. Counting on test day wastes time, so practise hitting 280 words by feel.

slides.title: The 4 Marking Criteria — what TR, CC, LR, GRA actually mean

cards.title: Task Response (TR)
cards.body: Are you answering the *exact* question asked? Is your position clear and consistent? Do you cover all parts (e.g. both sides of a discussion)? Word count under 250 = automatic TR penalty.

cards.title: Coherence & Cohesion (CC)
cards.body: Is the essay organised into clear paragraphs with one main idea each? Do ideas flow with linkers (*however, in addition, as a result*)? Can the examiner follow your logic without re-reading?

cards.title: Lexical Resource (LR)
cards.body: Is your word choice **accurate and appropriate** for the topic? Range matters, but precision matters more — using *sophisticated* words wrongly hurts more than using simple words correctly.

cards.title: Grammatical Range & Accuracy (GRA)
cards.body: Do you use a variety of structures (conditionals, passives, complex sentences) **and** keep them error-free? A complex sentence with three errors scores lower than a simple sentence done right.

slides.title: Each criterion is worth 25% of your Task 2 score.
slides.body: When you get feedback saying 'TR 6.5, CC 7, LR 6, GRA 7' — that's your scorecard against these four boxes. Every lesson in this guide trains one or more of them. We'll flag which criterion you're working on as we go.

lessons.title: Problem #1 — Misunderstanding the Question
lessons.liz_intro: Most candidates spot a keyword and start writing everything they know about that topic. That's the fastest way to wreck your Task Response score.
lessons.liz_outro: Identify the question type first. Now: structure.

items.title: Most candidates rush straight into writing without thoroughly analysing the question. They spot keywords and immediately begin writing everything they know about that topic, missing the specific requirements entirely.
**Critical insight:** IELTS questions have precise requirements. They're asking you to perform a specific task, not simply write about a general topic.

columns.label: Wrong Approach
items: Writing everything you know about those topics

columns.label: Missing Element
items: Failing to identify the question type
Cannot fulfil the actual task requirements

columns.label: Ultimate Result
items: If you don't answer the actual question being asked, nothing else you write matters
Your Task Response score will be severely limited

slides.title: Master Question Analysis
slides.intro: Before writing a single word, complete three essential steps that determine the success of your entire essay.

steps.title: Identify the Question Type
steps.body: Recognise whether it's opinion, discussion, problem-solution, advantages-disadvantages, or two-part.

steps.title: Underline Key Parts
steps.body: Mark the instruction words and specific focus areas that define your task.

steps.title: Decide Your Approach
steps.body: Determine exactly what you're being asked to do before planning your response.

slides.title: Three things — every prompt, no exceptions.
slides.body:
When the audit step says **'underline key parts'**, here's what to actually circle on the question paper:

**(1) The TASK words** — *agree/disagree, discuss, advantages, problems, solutions, why, to what extent.* These tell you the **question type** and the **structure** you must use.

**(2) The TOPIC noun phrase** — the *thing* the essay is about. *'Working from home', 'university tuition fees', 'public transport'.* Stay on this exact topic — don't drift into 'office work' or 'student loans'.

**(3) Any LIMITS or QUALIFIERS** — *young people, in developing countries, in the future, more than before.* Miss these and you answer the wrong question.

**Worked example:** *"Some people think that **young people** in **developed countries** should be **forced to do unpaid community work**. **To what extent do you agree or disagree?**"*

→ Task: opinion essay (agree/disagree).  
→ Topic: forced unpaid community work.  
→ Limits: young people / developed countries.  
→ If you write about adults in poor countries, you fail Task Response no matter how good your English is.

blocks.text: The question type fundamentally shapes your entire approach, structure, and content. Understanding this distinction is crucial:

cards.title: Opinion
cards.body:
*Signal:* "To what extent do you agree or disagree?" / "What is your opinion?"
*Example prompt:* **"Working from home is more productive than working in an office. To what extent do you agree or disagree?"**
→ Pick a side, defend it for 2 body paragraphs.

cards.title: Discussion (DBV)
cards.body:
*Signal:* "Discuss both views and give your own opinion."
*Example prompt:* **"Some people believe working from home boosts productivity, while others argue it weakens collaboration. Discuss both views and give your own opinion."**
→ Cover both sides fairly *then* state your stance.

cards.title: Problem-Solution
cards.body:
*Signal:* "What are the problems… and what solutions can be suggested?" / "Why is this happening and what can be done?"
*Example prompt:* **"In many cities, traffic congestion is worsening. What are the main causes, and what solutions can be suggested?"**
→ Pair each solution to a specific problem.

cards.title: Advantages-Disadvantages
cards.body:
*Signal:* "Discuss the advantages and disadvantages." / "Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?"
*Example prompt:* **"More and more people are choosing to work from home. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages."**
→ Two-sided analysis, no advocacy unless 'outweigh' wording asks.

cards.title: Two-Part / Double Question
cards.body:
*Signal:* Two distinct questions in the prompt.
*Example prompt:* **"Many young people choose careers based on income rather than passion. Why is this the case, and is it a positive or negative development?"**
→ Body 1 = answer Question 1; Body 2 = answer Question 2.

blocks.text: Each type demands a different structural approach and content focus.

lessons.title: Problem #2 — Poor Essay Structure
lessons.liz_intro: Either no structure at all, or some over-engineered framework from YouTube that falls apart under exam pressure. Neither works.
lessons.liz_outro: Simple structure. Now the part where most marks are lost: paragraph development.

blocks.text: Many candidates either lack any clear structure whatsoever, or they attempt to implement complicated frameworks found online that don't actually function effectively in exam conditions.
Your essay requires a **clear, logical structure** that the examiner can follow effortlessly. This means having the right components in the right places, with appropriate content in each section.
*The simple 4-paragraph model on the next slide is what an examiner expects to see at Band 7+. It's not a 'trick' — it's how clear academic argument is built.*

slides.title: Use a Simple, Reliable Structure
slides.intro: Stop searching for complicated structures. What consistently works is straightforward and proven.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.body: Paraphrase the question and briefly state your answer or position.

nodes.title: Main Body Paragraph 1
nodes.body: Present your first main idea and develop it fully with explanation and example.

nodes.title: Main Body Paragraph 2
nodes.body: Present your second main idea and develop it fully with explanation and example.

nodes.title: Conclusion
nodes.body: Summarise your main ideas and clearly restate your answer to the question.

lessons.title: Problem #3 — Weak Paragraph Development
lessons.liz_intro: This is where most marks are lost. Students give an idea, throw in an example, then move on — skipping the layer that actually scores.
lessons.liz_outro: Three layers, every paragraph. Now the full 9-step framework.

blocks.text: This is the **single biggest reason** Band-6 essays don't reach Band-7.
Students write a main idea, then immediately jump to the next point without properly explaining or supporting the first one. Or they provide examples that don't genuinely support their argument.

Some candidates attempt to include three or four ideas in one paragraph rather than developing a single idea comprehensively. The examiner wants to see **depth of development, not superficial breadth**.

slides.eyebrow: THE METAPHOR THAT MAKES IT CLICK
slides.title: Layer 2 is the BRIDGE between your idea and your example.
slides.body:
Look at the photo on the previous slide — a bridge connecting two banks. That's exactly what your **explanation (Layer 2)** does for the examiner:

• **Bank 1 = your main idea** (Layer 1, the topic sentence).  
• **Bank 2 = your example** (Layer 3, the concrete evidence).  
• **The bridge = your reasoning** that connects them.

Most students leap from Bank 1 straight to Bank 2 — *'Working from home increases productivity. For example, my friend works from home.'* The examiner has to *guess* the connection, and Coherence & Cohesion drops to Band 5.

Your bridge sentences answer: ***Why* does the idea cause the example?** Walk the examiner across the gap step by step.

slides.title: The Birthday Cake Strategy
slides.intro: Think of paragraph development like decorating a birthday cake. You don't simply slap on icing and call it finished — you build layers deliberately.

slides.title: Build Every Paragraph in Three Layers
slides.intro: Every paragraph needs all three layers in this order. Most students provide the main idea and example but skip the explanation — or give explanation without a clear example. You need all three.

steps.title: Layer 1 — The Cake
steps.body: Your main idea — the foundation of everything that follows.

steps.title: Layer 2 — The Icing
steps.body: **2–3 sentences** of explanation. Don't stop at 'this is important because it affects society' — that's the trap. Move from the abstract idea toward a concrete consequence: *'Why does this matter? → What happens because of it? → Who feels it?'* If your explanation could be slotted into any other essay, it's too generic. **This is your Coherence & Cohesion (CC) work** — examiners grade how well one idea develops into the next.

steps.title: Layer 3 — The Decorations
steps.body: Your specific example or evidence that demonstrates and supports your explanation.

columns.label: Jumping Too Quickly
items: Moving to the next main idea before fully developing the current one with proper explanation.

columns.label: Irrelevant Examples
items: Providing examples that don't actually support or illustrate the point being made.

columns.label: Multiple Ideas
items: Cramming three or four different ideas into one paragraph instead of developing one thoroughly.

columns.label: Missing Explanation
items: The most common error — giving main idea and example but omitting the crucial explanation layer.

lessons.title: The 9-Step Framework
lessons.liz_intro: Here's a process that reliably works — *because* you'll bend it to your question, not the other way around. Nine steps to give your essay a spine; you decide what flesh it carries.
lessons.liz_outro: That's the full framework. Now: how to actually apply it.

items.title: A process that reliably works for any Task 2 question type — because you'll adapt it. The nine steps give you a spine; the *thinking* is still yours. People learn by breaking scaffolds, not by clinging to them.

slides.title: Use the steps as a checklist, not a script.
slides.body: The 9 steps tell you **what to do**, not **what to write**. Adapt every step to your specific question. If a step feels forced, your idea is right and the formula is wrong — trust the idea.

slides.title: Plan Before You Write
slides.intro: **Critical reminder:** Don't skip the planning phase. Five minutes of planning will save you fifteen minutes of confused, unfocused writing.

steps.title: Read Carefully
steps.body: Read the question thoroughly, paying attention to every word and instruction.

steps.title: Identify Question Type
steps.body: Determine what type of question it is and precisely what it's asking you to do.

steps.title: Plan Two Main Ideas
steps.body: Plan your two main ideas — and only two main ideas. Don't try to cover everything.

cards.title: 1. Paraphrase the Question
cards.body: Rewrite the question in your own words — but **keep the meaning and tone identical**. *Trap:* over-paraphrasing changes what the question is asking. "Working from home is better" → "Remote labour arrangements optimise productivity" is wrong: you've shifted from a lifestyle claim to an economic one. Swap 2–3 nouns and verbs for synonyms; keep the proposition intact.

cards.title: 2. State Your Answer
cards.body: Answer the question briefly in one or two clear sentences. Tell the examiner exactly what your position is.

blocks.text: This isn't the time for explanation — that comes in the body paragraphs. This section is solely for clearly stating your answer or position.

slides.eyebrow: HOW TO STATE YOUR POSITION (BY QUESTION TYPE)
slides.title: Step 5 changes shape depending on the question type.
slides.body:
**Opinion** — *'In my view, [position] because [headline reason 1] and [headline reason 2].'*  
→ Pick a clear side. **No fence-sitting.**

**Discussion (DBV)** — *'While [side A] argue [headline], [side B] believe [headline]. **In my view, [your stance] is more convincing**, as the rest of this essay will show.'*  
→ **DBV position signal:** your stance MUST appear in the introduction, not just the conclusion. Examiners look for a clear opinion early.

**Advantages-Disadvantages (no 'outweigh')** — *'This essay will examine both [main advantage] and [main disadvantage].'*  
→ No stance — both sides equally.

**Adv-Dis ('outweigh')** — *'Although there are [headline disadvantage], the [headline advantages] are far more significant.'*  
→ Take a side ON the outweigh question.

**Problem-Solution** — *'This essay will identify [headline problem] and propose [headline solution].'*  
→ Pair-preview: each problem will get a matching solution.

**Two-Part** — *'This essay will explain [Q1 short answer] and argue that this is a [positive/negative] development.'*  
→ Both questions previewed, in order.

slides.title: Build Each Body Paragraph
slides.intro: Then repeat Steps 6–8 for your second main body paragraph. Two paragraphs, two main ideas, both fully developed with all three layers.

steps.title: First Main Idea
steps.body: Begin your first main body paragraph with your first main idea stated clearly in a topic sentence.

steps.title: Explain Thoroughly
steps.body: **Aim for 2–3 sentences** moving from idea → consequence. Generic explanations ("this is important for society") score Band 5. A Band-7 explanation tracks a chain: *cause → effect → who's affected*. Forces you to think — which is the whole point.

steps.title: Specific Example
steps.body: Provide a specific example that supports your explanation. Not vague — specific. Not "many people" — tell who, where, when, what happened.

slides.title: Where Students Lose Marks in Step 7

blocks.text: *Band-7 reference: this is the level you're aiming for. The technique below is what an examiner would expect to see at Band 7+.*
Step 7 is where most candidates struggle significantly. They don't explain enough — they state an idea and immediately jump to an example.
Your explanation needs to develop the idea thoroughly:

cards.title: Why does this matter?
cards.body: Why does this idea matter to the question?

cards.title: How does it connect?
cards.body: How does it connect to the broader topic?

cards.title: What are the implications?
cards.body: What are the consequences of this point?

cards.title: Why should they care?
cards.body: Why should the reader care about this point?

blocks.text: This explanation layer is what demonstrates **critical thinking** and earns higher band scores. Don't rush past it.

slides.title: Weak vs strong explanation, side by side
slides.intro: Same main idea, same example. The difference is **Step 7** — how the explanation walks the examiner from idea to evidence.

stimulus.label: Topic sentence (Step 6)
stimulus.text: **Main idea:** Working from home increases employee productivity.

question.label: Compare two versions of the explanation that follows the topic sentence

items.stem: **Weak (Band 5–6):** *'This is important for society. Many people prefer it. For example, my friend Tom works from home and he is very productive.'*

**Strong (Band 7+):** *'When employees skip a long commute, they reclaim 60–90 minutes a day that would otherwise be spent in traffic — time that often shifts directly into focused work in the morning. The home environment also removes the constant low-level interruptions of an open-plan office: drop-by questions, ambient conversation, impromptu meetings. Together, these two factors mean a remote worker can produce in five hours what an office worker produces in seven. For example, a 2023 Stanford study of 16,000 employees at a Fortune 500 firm found that those working from home completed 13% more tasks per week than their office-based counterparts.'*

thinking_steps: **Why the weak version fails:** It's three claims with no chain — it doesn't *explain* why working from home increases productivity. The example ('my friend Tom') is anecdotal and unverifiable. There is no reasoning to grade.

**What the strong version does:** It tracks a chain — *cause (no commute) → effect (recovered time) → mechanism (focused morning hours)* — then adds a second causal link (*home → no interruptions*) before the example.
**The example is specific:** Stanford / 16,000 employees / Fortune 500 / 13% more tasks. The example *supports* the explanation; it doesn't replace it.
**Length check:** Strong = 3 sentences of explanation, then 1 sentence of example. That's the Layer-2 / Layer-3 ratio Step 7 wants.

answer.value: Strong version = Band 7+ Task Response. Weak version caps at Band 5.5.
answer.explanation: The Step-7 explanation must answer **'why does the main idea cause this outcome?'** with a chain of reasoning, not a list of vague claims. If you can delete your explanation and the paragraph still makes the same point, your explanation is doing no work — rewrite it.

blocks.text: Summarise your two main ideas and clearly answer the question one final time.

cards.title: Don't introduce new information
cards.body: Save new ideas for body paragraphs.

cards.title: Don't write three long sentences
cards.body: Keep it tight.

cards.title: Don't repeat your introduction
cards.body: Word-for-word repetition wastes the examiner's time.

blocks.text: **Two concise sentences** is usually sufficient for an effective conclusion.

slides.title: Framework Summary
slides.intro: The whole system in five movements.

nodes.title: Pre-Writing (Steps 1–3)
nodes.body: Analyse question, identify type, plan two main ideas.

nodes.title: Conclusion (Step 9)
nodes.body: Summarise and clearly answer the question.

slides.title: Quick Check: The Writing Framework
slides.intro: Five questions to lock in the 9-step framework, the Birthday Cake Strategy, and the three-layer paragraph rule.

items.stem: Which step in the 9-step framework MUST come before you write a single sentence of the introduction?

options.text: Read the question carefully, identify the question type, and plan two main ideas.
Count your words to estimate timing.
Translate the prompt into your first language.

items.explanation: Steps 1–3 (read carefully, identify type, plan two ideas) are pre-writing. Skipping them is Problem #1 — misunderstanding the question.

items.stem: In the Birthday Cake Strategy, what does each layer represent?

options.text: Cake = vocabulary, icing = grammar, decorations = punctuation.
Cake = main idea, icing = explanation, decorations = specific example.
Cake = introduction, icing = body, decorations = conclusion.
Cake = Task 1, icing = Task 2, decorations = Speaking.

items.explanation: Layer 1 (Cake) = main idea / topic sentence. Layer 2 (Icing) = explanation. Layer 3 (Decorations) = specific example. Every body paragraph needs all three.

items.stem: How many main ideas should you plan for a Task 2 essay before writing? (one digit)
items.explanation: Two main ideas — one per body paragraph. More than two and you can't develop them in 250 words; one alone leaves the essay underdeveloped.

items.stem: A student writes a body paragraph that's just a topic sentence followed by three more general claims. What's missing?

options.text: The icing (explanation) and decorations (specific example) — only the cake is there.

items.explanation: This is Problem #3 — weak paragraph development. Topic sentence alone is only the cake. You need explanation + a specific example to score Band 7+ on Task Response.

items.stem: What's the right approach for the conclusion (Step 9)?

options.text: Introduce one new idea to show range.
Two concise sentences that summarise and clearly answer the question.
A 5-sentence paragraph repeating each body paragraph in detail.
Skip it if you're running out of time — examiners ignore conclusions.

items.explanation: Two concise sentences is usually sufficient — restate your answer and summarise. No new ideas, no padding, but never skip it: a missing conclusion costs marks on Task Response and Coherence.

lessons.title: Apply It Inside IELTS Ace
lessons.liz_intro: You've got the framework. Now the only thing that moves your score is **specific feedback on your specific essays**. Here's how IELTS Ace turns this guide into actual band gains.
lessons.liz_outro: That's it — system in your head, surfaces ready to use. Open the writing tab when you're ready to start.

slides.title: Where to Practise the Framework
slides.intro: You learnt the system. These are the surfaces inside IELTS Ace where you actually apply it — every essay you write here is graded against the same Band 7/8/9 criteria.

cards.title: Writing Question Bank
cards.body: Every Task 2 question type — opinion, discussion, problem-solution, advantages-disadvantages, two-part — with a fresh prompt each time.

cards.title: Per-Essay Sonnet Evaluation
cards.body: Submit any essay and get a band score against all four criteria, with the exact sentences you need to fix highlighted.

cards.title: Liz Writing Coach
cards.body: Stuck on Step 7 explanation? Liz reads your draft, names what's missing, and walks you through the rewrite.

cards.title: Mistake Analysis
cards.body: Your recurring writing mistakes are tracked across essays so you can practise the 3–4 errors actually holding your band back.

slides.title: Diagnose AND Fix — In One Week

blocks.text: A one-off diagnostic tells you what's wrong. The IELTS Ace **Weekly Pack** does the diagnosis *and* gives you the seven days of guided practice to actually fix it.

cards.title: Write Your First Essay
cards.body: Use the framework you just learnt. Submit it inside the app.

cards.title: Sonnet Grades It
cards.body: Calibrated to IELTS examiner descriptors — not inflated scores. You get a band, the four-criteria breakdown, and the exact sentences losing marks.

cards.title: 20 Liz Sessions to Fix It
cards.body: A full week of unlimited writing eval + 20 Liz coaching messages to rewrite your weakest paragraphs and lock in the framework.

blocks.text: **Why Weekly beats a one-off diagnostic:** clarity is only useful if you act on it within days, while the patterns are still fresh. A week of guided rewrites is what actually moves your band.

slides.title: Specific Feedback. Not Generic Advice.
slides.intro: Most students waste months practising the wrong things. They repeat the same mistakes because nobody has shown them specifically what's wrong with their writing. The Weekly Pack eliminates the guesswork.

cards.title: Where You're Losing Marks
cards.body: The specific bands and exact sentences where your writing isn't meeting requirements.

cards.title: Hidden Mistakes
cards.body: Errors you're repeating that you don't even realise are problems.

cards.title: What to Focus On
cards.body: The 3–4 fixes that will move your band the fastest — prioritised, not generic.

slides.title: It's the difference between hoping you're improving and knowing you're improving in the right direction.

items.title: Most students waste months on the wrong things. A week of specific, evaluator-graded feedback beats months of unsupervised practice.

slides.title: Recommended Next Steps
slides.intro: That's how you actually improve — not by watching endless random videos, but by getting specific feedback on your specific mistakes and practising strategically.

steps.title: Review This Framework
steps.body: Re-read this material until all nine steps feel automatic.

steps.title: Write Your First Essay
steps.body: Open the Writing Question Bank and apply the framework on a real Task 2 prompt.

steps.title: Get Your Sonnet Evaluation
steps.body: See where you stand against the four criteria and which 3–4 errors are costing you the most.

steps.title: Take the Weekly Pack
steps.body: Seven days of guided rewrites + Liz coaching to actually fix what the evaluation surfaced.

slides.title: What to do, day by day.
slides.body:
**Day 1 — Diagnose.**  Open the Writing Question Bank, pick a Task 2 prompt, and write a full essay using the 9 steps. Submit it for Sonnet evaluation. Read the band breakdown twice.  

**Day 2 — Triage.**  From your evaluation, pick the **3 lowest-scoring sentences**. Ask Liz: *'How would a Band-7 candidate rewrite this sentence?'* Save her rewrites in the Vocabulary Tracker.

**Day 3 — Rebuild ONE paragraph.**  Take the weakest body paragraph from Day 1. Rewrite it using **Birthday Cake layers** (cake → icing → decorations). Submit just the paragraph for a quick Liz check.

**Day 4 — New essay, same question type.**  Pick a different prompt of the *same type* (e.g., another opinion essay). Apply Day 1–3 lessons. Submit. Compare scores.

**Day 5 — Switch question type.**  Try a different question type (DBV, Adv-Dis, etc.). Use the question-type cards from Step 5 to set up your introduction.

**Day 6 — Mistake review.**  Open Mistake Analysis. Look for the 1–2 errors that appear in *every* essay. Drill them with Liz.

**Day 7 — Full timed essay.**  40 minutes, no help. Submit. The score from Day 7 vs Day 1 is your real progress signal.

**Then repeat with new prompts.** This is how Band 6 becomes Band 7.
